coredata.dc:title: A sensing approach for manganese ion detection by carbon dots nanocomposite thin film-based surface plasmon resonance sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Omar, Nur Alia Sheh
Irmawati, Ramli
Fen, Yap Wing
Abdullah, Jaafar
Daud, Nor Faezah Mohd
Daniyal, Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd
Mahdi, Mohd Adzir
coredata.dc:description: Although plenty of carbon dots have been synthesized by many kinds of synthesis methods, their potential in metal sensing applications is still limited. Herein, the application of carbon dots-cellulose nanocrystals (CDs-CNC) thin film towards manganese ions sensing was demonstrated experimentally using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor. The experimental results showed that the CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor provides the detection of parts per billion concentrations of Mn2+ in an aqueous solution with high sensitivity response of 0.0534° ppb-1. The thickness and refractive index of thin films were then determined by fitting the experimental SPR spectra using the WinSpall simulation program. The proposed CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor exhibit better sensing performance in comparison to the previous film-based sensor, suggesting the suitable technology candidate for environmental monitoring applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Manganese ions
Surface plasmon resonance
Optical detection

Spin coating of CDs-CNC thin film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the CDs synthesis using hydrothermal method followed by visualization of CDs under daylight and UV light.
A schematic of custom-made SPR experimental setup.
SPR signal of CDs-CNC thin film with different concentrations (4–20 ppb) of Mn2+.
Fitting experimental data to theoretical data for CDs-CNC thin film with different concentrations (4–20 ppb) of Mn2+.
A plot of resonance angle shift for CDs-CNC thin film with different concentrations of Mn2+.
FWHM, DA, and SNR for CDs-CNC thin film with different concentrations (4–20 ppb) of Mn2+.
The resonance angle shift of the Au/CDs-CNC thin film exposed to different concentrations of Mn2+.
The optical parameters of the proposed sensor film when exposed to different concentrations of Mn2+.
The values of FWHM, DA, and SNR for Au/CDs-CNC thin film exposed to different concentrations of Mn2+.
Performance comparison of the CDs-CNC thin film with other materials based optical sensor for Mn2+ detection.

head.ce:title: A sensing approach for manganese ion detection by carbon dots nanocomposite thin film-based surface plasmon resonance sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although plenty of carbon dots have been synthesized by many kinds of synthesis methods, their potential in metal sensing applications is still limited. Herein, the application of carbon dots-cellulose nanocrystals (CDs-CNC) thin film towards manganese ions sensing was demonstrated experimentally using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor. The experimental results showed that the CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor provides the detection of parts per billion concentrations of Mn2+ in an aqueous solution with high sensitivity response of 0.0534° ppb-1. The thickness and refractive index of thin films were then determined by fitting the experimental SPR spectra using the WinSpall simulation program. The proposed CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor exhibit better sensing performance in comparison to the previous film-based sensor, suggesting the suitable technology candidate for environmental monitoring applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Some metal ions are crucial components of the living organism. Manganese is one of the essential microelements in many cellular metabolisms that crucial for normal body functioning and developments [1,2]. Additionally, manganese has also grown as one of the indispensable raw materials in industrial production. Up to 90% of manganese consumption is accounted by the steel industry in deoxidizing and desulfurizing additives and as an alloying constituent [3]. Although manganese has many benefits roles in human activities, when it released into the environment even in low concentrations, will accumulate and exert their toxic effects on living organisms over a long time. The uptake of manganese by humans mainly takes place through food such as grains and rice, soya beans, eggs, nuts, and oysters [4]. Also, the presence of manganese in the water system contributes to the formation of oxide layers in corroded pipes. As a result, the water flow is affected as manganese is objectionable in water even present in smaller concentrations than iron [5,6]. Excessive manganese consumption in drinking water (≥0.3 ppm) may cause dullness, weak muscles, and headaches, which could potentially lead to a Parkinson-like syndrome, lung embolism, and bronchitis [7,8]. Compared with other valence states of manganese, Mn2+ ions are stable in environmental water.

Recently, a large number of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors have been established to detect manganese ions at low concentrations [9–12]. Unfortunately, a few or no works of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors are reported for Mn2+ detection [13]. Following this report, it is of our interest to explore the application of SPR sensor for Mn2+ detection. To measure the extremely low ppb level of Mn2+ with high sensitivity, a high refractive index material based SPR thin film is required.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have been increasingly used in sensors and biosensors design due to their unique physicochemical properties. These materials include carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, carbon nanofibers, graphene and its derivatives, graphene oxide, nanodiamonds, and carbon-based quantum dots. Interestingly, carbon dots (CDs) have been emerged as the most precious gift in nanotechnology because of their significant advantages in terms of low toxicity, chemical inertness, good water solubility, and physicochemical properties [14–16]. CDs are typically carbon nanoparticles, defined by characteristics sizes of less than 10 nm. In the last few years, a variety of methods has been utilized to synthesize CDs, including from both natural and synthetic organic precursors [17]. The most promising method is hydrothermal since its operations are easy to use, low-cost, and environment friendly [18,19]. On the other hand, the ubiquitous use of cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) in diverse fields such as biomedical engineering, material sciences, and electronics has drawn great interest in compositing with CDs. The competitive advantages of CNC in terms of ease of fabrication, large surface area, high strength and stiffness, and lack of apparent toxicity has propelled a vast array of research works in making conductive composites for sensor applications [20,21].

The primary objective of this study is to evaluate the potential detection of Mn2+ using CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor. To the best of our knowledge, there is no such report available so far. With this insight, here, the resonance angle and other performance parameters, i.e. sensitivity, full width at half maximum, detection accuracy were experimentally calculated. The optical properties of CDs-CNC thin film were further evaluated using the WinSpall program to validate the experimental results.

Citric acid anhydrous, hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTA), and manganese ions solution (1000 ppm) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were purchased from CelluForce while ethylenediamine (EDA) was purchased from Bio-Basic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and CNC solution
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to most investigated CDs, these CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method ( Fig. 1). Briefly, 3 g of citric acid as carbon source was mixed with EDA and then were dissolved in deionized water. The mixture was transferred into a teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 150 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The obtained dark brown CDs solution was then purified using a dialysis bag. To prepare CNC, a previous method reported by Abitbol et al. was used, where 5 g of CNC was diluted in deionized water (0.1 wt% suspensions) and then was modified with 0.1 wt% CTA. The modified CNC was stirred vigorously at room temperature, followed by a centrifuging process of 8000 rpm for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spin coating of CDs-CNC thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Approximately 0.50 ml of CDs-CNC composite solution (1:1 ratio) was dropped onto gold film covering its surface. The composite solution was left to rest for 2 mins before spinning using Spin Coating System, P-6708D. The spin coating parameters were set at 6000 rpm for 30 s with an acceleration of 2 ms-2.

Kretschmann model was exhibited to define the principle of SPR biosensors including experimental setups of He-Ne laser beam, a rotating stage driven by a stepper motor, a polarizer, an optical chopper, a o-ring, a photodiode, and a lock-in amplifier ( Fig. 2). The SPR experiment was then initiated by evaporating a derivative sensor film on the prism surface and placed on a rotating stage for angle scanning. After the setup was ready, 1 ml of deionized water (DW) was injected into the o-ring to obtain the baseline signal. The DW was then injected out for the next use of different concentrations of Mn2+ solution. The target solution was injected one after another into the o-ring to be in contact with the sensor surface. Finally, the SPR signal was imaged through a computer, resulted in the SPR curve. The SPR peak angular position and intensity were very sensitive to the optical properties of the media within the evanescent field. All SPR signals obtained from the interaction were then fitted to the theoretical SPR reflectance curve obtained from mathematical analysis (Fresnel equations and transfer matrix method) in order to calculate the optical properties of thin films [22]. In this fitting, the Wispall software program was used.

The efficiency and sensitivity of the Au/CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor was examined for the quantitative detection of Mn2+ ions in an aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 3, the introduction of 4 ppb Mn2+ solution on Au/CDs-CNC thin film surface has shifted the SPR angle of DW to the rightward. The corresponding SPR angle for DW and 4 ppb Mn2+ solution is found to be 53.4406° and 53.5674°, respectively. The Au/CDs-CNC thin film surface was then allowed to contact with different concentrations of Mn2+ ions solution, i.e. 6 ppb, 8 ppb, 10 ppb, and 20 ppb, and the resulting SPR responses were recorded in Table 1. The results showed that the shift of SPR angle increased with increasing Mn2+ concentration up to 10 ppb and remained constant for 20 ppb. The increase in the shift of SPR angle is mainly due to the attachment of Mn2+ ions onto Au/CDs-CNC thin film surface. This means that when the sensing layer is exposed to the Mn2+ ions solution, the chemical absorption will induce a change in the sensing layer’s refractive index, which changes the position of SPR angle. It is believed that the change of SPR angle is associated with the change of the refractive index real part of the sensor surface. Other than resonance angle shift, it is also worth noting that the minimum reflectance has a slight change due to changes in the imaginary part and thickness of the sensor layer as the Mn2+ concentrations were varied.

To validate this observation, the experimental SPR results were fitted using the WinSpall program ( Fig. 4). The complex refractive index of the gold layer was obtained when contacted deionized water (refractive index = 1.3313), with n and k values of 0.1700 + 3.8139i and the thickness of 46.4 nm. This value is in good agreement with the one reported in [23]. The determination of these values thus allows further finding for refractive index real part (n) and imaginary part (k) and the thickness of the CDs-CNC sensing layer. In this case, it is good to conclude that the real part of the refractive index of the sensing layer increases as the concentration of Mn2+ ions increase, which is opposite to the imaginary part. This is due to the strong interaction between the evanescent fields and sensing medium, which in turn increases the real part of the wave vector of the surface plasmon wave (ksp). The real part of ksp will be large when the refractive index is large to meet the resonance condition at the larger valuer of the SPR angle [24]. Similarly, the observed effect of the binding can be attributed to the increase in CDs-CNC sensing layer thickness as shown in Table 2. On the other hand, the imaginary part of the refractive index is slightly reduced leading to less energy loss due to electronic damping.

To further analyze the performance of the SPR sensor towards sensitivity, the linear regression analysis of the Au/CDs-CNC thin film was performed in the Mn2+ concentrations range of 0–10ppb as shown in Fig. 5. The Mn2+ concentration of 20 ppb was neglected in this analysis because it was placed far beyond the linearly fitted line, which may result in a poor linear response. The sensitivity for the SPR sensor is highly dependent on the shift of SPR angle obtained from the ligand-analyte interaction. It was observed that the proposed Au/CDs-CNC thin film had a good linear response (R2 = 0.98) with a gradient value (which defined as sensitivity) of 0.0534° ppb-1.

Besides sensitivity, full width at half maximum (FWHM) is also an important parameter, which the value should be as small as possible. A smaller value of FWHM implies a narrow and sharp SPR curve, signifying a better detection accuracy of measurements. The variations of FWHM as well as DA and SNR with respect to Mn2+ ions concentrations were depicted in Fig. 6. It is observed that with the introduction of 4–8 ppb of Mn2+ ions, the FWHM decreases, revealing the increases of detection accuracy. However, when a higher concentration of Mn2+ ions was introduced, the detection accuracy becomes weaker due to the poor adsorption capability. It can be explained by the increase in the thickness of the sensing layer, which leads to damping of incident light in the sensing layer, thus broadening the SPR curve [25–27]. When the shift of SPR angle is multiplied with DA, the SNR of the Au/CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor can be obtained as shown in Fig. 5. The SNR of the proposed sensor was found to increase with increasing Mn2+ concentrations, suggesting a relative increase in signal strength as the binding of Mn2+ ions was maximized onto the sensor surface. The overall performance parameter analysis of the proposed sensor against Mn2+ ions concentrations was given in Table 3.

From Table 4, it is observed that this proposed Au/CDs-CNC thin film-based SPR sensor is highly sensitive to detect the lowest Mn2+ concentration compared to other affinity layer-based optical sensors. Basically, the reason for the lowest detectable concentration of Mn2+ is that, the development of this SPR sensor, where Au plays the role as an active metal to generate surface plasmon, CDs and CNC act as a composite sensing layer to provide more active site for the attachment of Mn2+ ions.

In this work, a novel SPR sensor based on Au/CDs-CNC thin film has been successfully developed for the potential detection of Mn2+. The experimental studies of SPR have been done to evaluate the resonance angle shift, sensitivity, full width at half maximum, detection accuracy, and signal-to-noise-ratio in line with increasing Mn2+ concentrations. The results showed that the proposed sensor had a positive response toward low concentrations of Mn2+ with a sensitivity of 0.0534° ppb-1. The lowest detectable concentration of Mn2+ of 4 ppb was also obtained, thus suggesting that this SPR sensor has better detection compared to other developed optical sensors. The optical properties of Au/CDs-CNC thin film and different concentrations of Mn2+ has also been studied, confirming the experimental results.

This research was funded through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) (FRGS/1/2019/STG02/UPM/02/1) and Putra Grant (GPPI 9476700) by Ministry of Education Malaysia and Universiti Putra Malaysia.

Nur Alia Sheh Omar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft preparation. Ramli Irmawati, Yap Wing Fen, Jaafar Abdullah, Mohd Adzir Mahdi: Validation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Nor Faezah Mohd Daud, Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd Daniyal: Visualization. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The laboratory facilities provided by the Faculty of Engineering, Institute of Advanced Technology, and Department of Chemistry, Universiti Putra Malaysia are acknowledged.
